sentences.text: As we arrived to Brunnlitz we were welcomed by Mr.
Schindler with arms spread, welcomed.
And told us that a good soup was waiting for us after we were relieved from standing.
We had a good kitchen staff in Schindler's place.
And we received good nourishment.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The accommodation in the beginning wasn't the perfect one, but later on everybody got his stand where he could sleep.
We were also having a washroom, a possibility of shaving, and keep clean.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We were located all sort of work.
I was, as usually, with the transport group.
There was a small, narrow line wagons, which we used to use to bring coal, vegetable from the railways into the kitchen, and also some vegetable.
Sometimes we had to clean some areas with [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE].
But generally speaking, we had a free-of-fear life.
Some people were employed in the machine rooms.
They prepared some tools.
They were-- first we had to remove the old machinery, bring the new machinery, because we were ordered to prepare some part of the new weapons.
We did not know, yet, exactly, but it was taking a lot of time before we received the material.
They have to bring heavy machinery from outside.
And these machinery were preparing some part of the new weapon.
I think it was the name of the new weapon, V2.
But strictly speaking, we only were in the time of preparation.
Nothing came as a good, final product.
We were preparing some boxes for this and some paint, because it had to have a color, gun paint.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But we were very close to start the production when the war ended.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And if you are interested to know what were the conditions, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we have that exactly-- you mentioned in the book written by Thomas Keneally.
And I think in the film of Spielberg, also, we, we see part of work in Schindler's place.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Generally speaking, if you allow me, I will repeat some words, which I remember from reading the books.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] If you allow me to.
To mention the general Schindler's, we were hungry, but not starving.
We were cold, but not freezing.
We had fear, but we were not beaten.
This is the true fact.
This is one remarkable distinction that some families emerged intact.
There were no hangings in Schindler's list.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] You had to have a very strong will to keep going.
Some people died because they couldn't stand the conditions.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] As long as you got a piece of bread and soup, you waited what was next to happen tomorrow.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] That was one only camp what we have seen from the history, shining in the Holocaust.
And we were very happy when the end of the war came, and Schindler told us, now you are free.
You can do what you want.
You can go what you want.
No, I don't remember anything like this.
No.
We heard about them, but they were not working with us.
No, exactly I had the old information.
And we were returned back to Poland.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were no communications.
But my brother, Joseph, was working in the storeroom in Schindler's camp.
There was a hidden radio.
And we had all information from the front.
We knew every step what's going on in the Europe area.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when the end was so close, Schindler allowed us to listen, officially, through radio the speech of Churchill.
We knew, now, that Europe is going to remodel the situation, that Hitler dreamt-- that Hitler's dream was really finished.
He wanted to build a 1,000 years emporium.
And after seven or eight years, he collapsed.
No, you only heard some broken information from the areas who were liberated.
And the press knew about it.
And the radio.
Because we heard this information from the radio of the storeroom.
No, but we knew that underground was existing.
There were contacts with them.
We know that German ordered to prepare behind the camp, a large tip where they wanted to finish us in the last moment.
The underground, the Czechoslovakian underground, kept a close watch.
They were ready for everything if that would happen.
But unfortunate-- fortunately it didn't come to that.
Not in Schindler's camp.
I don't remember.
There was only one Friday, I remember, a kiddush was done because the rabbi Levertov fortunately was there.
And Schindler allowed them to, on the sabbath, to remember this.
I can't remember any other Jewish holy days.
Maybe there was some groups who were close, but I wasn't involved in that.
No, we had only outside [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] armies-- members of the Army.
But they were not interested in what's going on inside.
Schindler had an exclusive right to be the one and only person who directs the factory, the production.
Of course, he communicated with the army commander, but we, we never had anything to do with it.
I think the name was Leopold, Leopold.
The guards were German.
I didn't see any others.
They were only in army of German Wehrmacht.
Yes, we know that the [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] high command decided to allow Marshal Konev army to liberate our camp.
And Schindler decided not to be liberated by the Russians, because they were-- information that he could be separated from us.
So he decided with a group of eight people to leave the camp a night before.
They were clothed in special striped uniforms, in order to remove any threat from unexpected things.

He received, also, a letter signed by three people by Stern, Salpeter, and somebody else, which will-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] certified that he is the liberator of us, that he has received-- he has to receive all help and be saved.
And when they left, they of course-- they were close to the American lines.
And everything, then, as we know from the books, was Germany, Israel.
And the fact the he expressed the wish to be buried in Israel speaks for itself.
What do you think supported you, sustained you?
Do you think-- was it your Jewish faith that supported you to survive?
Yes, I was a strong believer.
And without having from anybody security promise, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I thoughts in my prayers that Almighty will take care about me and my family, that we will-- in the end, we will reunite.
And we will survive the Holocaust.
When you worked for Schindler, did-- who allocated the type of work you were going to do?
Oh, there was an organization in our administration according to the occupation we were registered.
People worked always in, in that area they can be used.
That was not compulsory, but that was satisfaction if you could work in your occupation.
There was no force which could change that what Schindler decided.
He decided to be with us all the time, until the end.
He had, of course, a position in circles.
He was even twice arrested.
But he had a strong contract with Berlin, and he was always made free.
And we believed in his word, without any hesitation.
There was time after the war that we said, if we can believe in God, our God was Schindler.
Yes, I think his bringing up in his family was of that sort.
What-- they say you have to love your neighbor like you love yourself.
And he understood that German-- Germany has done a great [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] mistake starting with the Jewish Final Solution.
That was a very [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] difficult thing that they undertook.
Of course, Eichmann was the head of that, Final Solution.
Eventually, he was caught up after the war and brought to the court in Israel and punished by hanging.
First of all, we have seen that on the railway we have seen a lot of transport with the Red Cross, with injured people.
And we found that the same wagon which we left yesterday to the left, they came back to the right, because they were limiting their expansion.
So we knew that something is wrong, after all, the radio give us the information.
We had no other way, because the newspaper didn't come to us.
I will tell you the truth, that it was rather a London, a famous London Voice of, of-- but there was also Voice of America, of course.
But maybe that during listening, there was a chance to listen to some German, Berlin, and maybe there was also a Moscow station available, because it was a strong radio.
Of course it was.
But it was prepared in such a way, faultless, that nobody could know about it.
First person who came to our camp was a soldier on a very miserable horse.
And he told us that the war has ended, that now you can be free and do what you wanted, go where you wanted.
And later on they came in larger amounts.
They registered us.
They gave us food, a lot of meat and everything.
We started to live like normal.
It took months before we were put in wagons again and sent back to Cracow.
That was the army of Marshal Konev.
But we don't know the names--
Oh, yes.
The Russian Army.
Oh, the German guards, after that Churchill speech, and after Schindler was-- also said goodbye to us, they decided to go back to, to their homes, because they knew that the war is finished.
And some of them even dropped their guns.
Yes.
Yes, yes.
They were there listening, because it was official now, by everybody.
Schindler was already on the way to the American lines.
He wasn't there.
He didn't want to be liberated by the Russians.
No, all German guards left.
Nobody wanted to keep contact with the Russians.
According to the-- Keneally's book, there were some motorcycles heard, and they came even for some petrol to go ahead.
And they received from the-- our-- from our camp petrol to go away.
Schindler left a night before we heard that they are close to our camp.
Probably after three or five days they were very far away.
And the Russians were not interested to go and search for him, because they asked us if we were volunteers.
And we said no, we were compelled to be here by German.
But Schindler helped us to survive.
No, we stayed where we were.
We registered.
We, we had our food.
We had a chance to go around.
And told us that a date will be announced where we can go back by train to Poland.
Oh, in the beginning we were very weak.
But if we started to use all possible food, sorry I can't continue, I have to go to the toilet.
If I-- [DRINKS]
When we were liberated, we decided to find out if that will be possible for us to live again in Poland.
But we found that the climate, how we call it, was completely different after the war.
They asked, the Russian men, how is the Cracow, how is the Poland?
And he said, everything is all right.
You will find for yourself.
So when we came, we found, really, Cracow was untouched by war.
There were no damages whatsoever.
And later on, after we settled sometime, we found that will be possible to go with the aliyah to Israel.
One holy day time, July or August, we received the information, which came from the foreign embassy, that a great immigration to Israel will be available.
So we registered ourselves, and later on, we went to.
But not singularly, only with a group.
But what I wanted to mention that during our return to Poland, we heard that Amon Goeth was-- How do you spell the name, please?
Amon Goeth, A-M-O-N G-O-E-T-H
He was the commander of Plaszow.
And he was imprisoned by Americans.
And later on, he was transferred to Polish authorities, who decided to try him.
It was a great trial.
I had even a ticket for this, but unfortunately I wasn't completely fit to, to be at the trial.
But I had the report.
He was hanged in Cracow jail.
But when we're talking about Goeth, I have to mention that I had two encounters with Amon Goeth.
One encounter was one day when I was working with a group of Jewish people by that time, in the stonework.
That was a day when Schindler visited us with his girlfriend together.
And Goeth called me, why the man is not working solidly?
And I mentioned, first of all, that he had returned from the sick, sick bed from Krankenstube, they call it, or [INAUDIBLE], or hospital.
And he-- it's not possible now.
He had to work strongly.
In that case, Goeth started to-- right hand.
And he wanted to reach the gun.
If he-- and at that moment, Schindler took his one arm.
And the girl took Goeth's second arm and they took him away, because they knew, immediately, it'll be a shooting.
That was the one encounter.
The other encounter was when I came with a group of people to the kitchen to collect soup.
There was a general order when somebody saw the commander approaching, that he should stand at, at-- how to call it-- remove his cap and call, achtung or at-- that means that the commander is coming.
When he was passing me through the door, he hit me in my face.
That is not necessary during the lunchtime to call, achtung.
That was only just an excuse, because whether it was done or not done, he wanted to hit me.
But it wasn't a great thing.
I survived it.
In these wagons which we had received from Brunnlitz, from Gross-Rosen.
The same wagons were for us.
Oh, probably it was a Czechoslovakian machinist.
I don't know.
But anyway, we were traveling without any guard.
We could take into our wagon everything what we wanted.
We didn't have much.
It was only one rucksack with some personal things.
It is worthwhile to mention that Schindler provided for us with some material for clothing and other things, which were amassed in the-- in the storeroom, so that everybody had something to keep.
I remember from that material I could make one suit and overcoat.
And from wool, the girls started to prepare some cardigans, who was clever enough.
In some cases, we received some cigarettes, some drinks.
And I heard, also, that Schindler provided arms for us, in case if we were, in the last moment, surrounded by the German, that we had arms to defend ourselves.
But it never came to it.
No, we knew that he went to Americans.
And later on, there came messages by correspondence that he is in Germany, and that Jews are caring for him.
He was that-- by that time, sick.
They arranged for him some medical.
We knew that later on we were collecting some money for Mrs.
Schindler.
I received even from her letter a letter to thank for the money which we sent.
But she was cared by higher authorities.
We know that she received a, a widow pension from Germany.
And the B'nai Brith arranged for her a house, as long as she lives.
She was provided, after all, she hasn't got a great demand now.
She's over 90.
When I arrived to Cracow on the 1st of June, and I came with my mother, with my sister, with my brother-in-law-- when we came we had information that my wife survived.
And she is on the way back to Cracow.
There were means of communication in the Jewish help in Cracow.
